Alright, you're back. It's time to rock and roll into my favorite module, Looping. Let's get into the specifics. It starts with the fact that regardless of what product you're selling and whether you're selling it in person or over the phone or even online, when you ask for the order for the very first time, there are only three possible ways your prospect can respond. First, they can say yes, because you got one of those perfect lay down sales, very rare. We get to close the sale without even a single objection. It's basically your lucky day, right? Second, they can say no, which means the prospect is definitely not interested. It's time to end that encounter as quickly as possible and move on to the next prospect. And just by the way here, you'll almost never get an outright no or I'm not interested at this point because you've already weeded out these prospects during the intelligence gathering phase. In other words, the only prospects you should still be speaking to at this point are the ones who made it past your intelligence gathering, namely the buyers in heat and the buyers in power. You weeded out those dreaded looky-loos and the mistakes during your transition, right? See, by definition, buyers in heat and buyers in power are those prospects who answered your intelligence gathering questions in a way that showed they were both interested in your product and could also afford your product. So given they just ticked those two boxes, the chance that they suddenly would now say, no, I don't want it. And if you just present them with an airtight logical case that hit their pain points, that's going to be extremely rare. You get it. So when you do get one, all it means is one of those looky-loos, not the mistakes. Those are usually easy to weed out, but the looky-loos had slipped through your intelligence gathering dreadnet, so to speak, which again is why you want to end that encounter as quickly as possible and move on to the next one. So again, they can say yes, which means you have one of those perfect, rare laydown sales, which in most industries is about, not 5% of the time, or they can say no, which for the reasons I just explained with the looky-loos and mistakes is typically even more rare than that. And then number three, they can also say maybe, as in maybe I'll buy and maybe I won't, which is basically a catch-all phrase for all the common objections you typically hear in the back half of the sale. Let me think about it, let me call you back, etc. Now the key thing to remember at this point is that on your initial closing attempt, you're fully expecting to get hit with one of the common objections. So you're in a monologue to be saying, ah, just as expected, a smoke screen for uncertainty. It's time to roll up my sleeves and earn my paycheck, as opposed to, oh no, my god, another objection, any penny, the sky's falling, I was praying for a laydown, I have to deal with another person who wants to think about it, all the humanity, what am I going to do? I hope I'm exaggerating a bit, but you get the point, right? But here's the irony of the whole thing. See, it doesn't matter which of these so-called common objections your prospect initially hits you with, you're always going to respond to it exactly the same way, to all of them in the same way. For example, let's stay with Bill again as our prospect, right? And I want to really slow things down here for you for the next few minutes, take you the step-by-step to make sure you understand the psychology of what's going on here. So let's say I ask for the or for the first time using a typical straight line closing pattern like, you know, and believe me, Bill, you're going to be very, very impressed. Not fair enough, that reasonable man turned the end, right? And in response to that, Bill replies, that sounds interesting, let me think about it. So to that, you're going to answer that so-called objection using the standard straight line response to every single initial objection, which is, I hear what you're saying, Bill, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Now similarly, if Bill had said to you, I want to speak to my accountant, and you would have said, I hear what you're saying, Bill, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Same thing, right? And once again, if he'd said, it's a bad time of year, he'd say, I hear what you're saying, Bill, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? In other words, no matter which of the objections he hits you with or she hits you with, you're always going to answer it in exactly the same way. You're going to say, I hear what you're saying, Bill or Jill or John or Jim or Angel or God knows who, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Now one important point before you start to panic, if you happen to be selling something like automobiles or TVs or clothing or any product doesn't quite fit into that box, meaning those exact words, just be flexible. In other words, with a little bit of tweaking, you could take that language pattern as simple as saying, so what do you think of the TV? Or what do you think? So in the back end, in the resource center again, because looping is very complex, I'm going to put extra stuff in there, I'm going to go through a bunch of different examples of how you would essentially adapt that pattern so if it's seamlessly into your industry, it's very easy to do, so don't worry, don't go crazy. It works for everyone. Now the key here, what you really focus on is that with this first pattern, what I did was that rather than directly answering my prospects objection, I deflected it instead. You get it? In other words, you're not going to actually answer this initial objection head on, I deflected. You deflected. Specifically, you acknowledged the fact that you heard what your prospects said to ensure they don't feel like they're annoyed, which caused them to break for poor and deal, right? And then you shift the conversation to a far more productive track, which is to find out where they stand on the certainty scale for the first of the three 10s, which of course is your product. Now again, straight line parlance, we refer to this process as deflection. And it's step number seven of the straight line syntax. Let's put the syntax up right here. Here are your seven steps. Step number one, you take immediate control. Step number two, use that control to gather intelligence. Step three, as you gather intelligence, you build master rapport using your active listening. Step four, when you're done gathering intelligence, you make your transition. I'm going fast here. Step number five, you make that awesome straight line presentation with framing and dot-frontloading. And you ask for the order for the first time. And boom, here we are. You get your first objection and you deflect it. You get it? Now, here's the way things work. It's really a two-step process here, this deflective pattern here, because I want to divide it into tonal sections here. In other words, the overall pattern is, I hear what you're saying, Bill, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like it? That's your pattern, right? But I want to divide it into two different chunks. Because the first part, I hear what you're saying, Bill, right? You're using this reasonable man-tonality. Essentially infuse with that. See what you're saying, Bill? Yes, I get it. I hear what you're saying. We are not breaking rapport. It's a reasonable thing you're saying. Not like, well, I hear what you're saying, Bill. You'll be like, whoa. And being you break rapport, you get it so you don't want to do that. Or even a dismissive tone, like, well, I hear what you're saying, Bill. Just like you're dismissing the guy's objection, right? Or again, said the right way, I hear what you're saying, Bill, which implies that you find your prospect rejection totally reasonable. See what's happening here is that your words are letting the prospect go. That you heard his objection, and that you're not ignoring him, and your tonality lets him know or her know that you fully respect their right to feel this way, which ensures that you're making an ultra-tight rapport. And step two of this process, or should I say tonally step two of this process, right? It consists of the second half of that language pattern. You're not going to hook on here, which is, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And you've done fused that with your money-assigned hypothetical tonality that you learned in the advanced tonality section. In other words, if you would hit Bill with a aggressive old bill, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? No, because if he says, yeah, you're going to bam, ram it down his throat by saying, you know, money-assigned, hypothetically speaking, you know, all things being equal, do you like what I'm offering? Does it make sense to you? And by doing that, you make it very easy for Bill to say, yeah, sure, why not? You get a more honest, accurate response. So altogether, what this sounds like this pattern is, I hear what you're saying, Bill, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And again, your words are redirecting the conversation to a more productive track, which is essentially to ascertain Bill's current level of certainty about your product. Right? See, up until this point, you didn't know, right? We don't know where our prospect lands on the certainty scale. Well, guess what? Now you've finally found out by saying, does it make sense? Do you like the idea? He goes, yeah, it sounds great. You're like, ooh, Bill's pretty high on the certainty scale. Or if I'd say, well, it's okay, Bill is lower. You understand the point is though, is that if you pressure him here with a heavy tonality, he's actually not going to give you an honest, responsible, downgrade his own enthusiasm because he's scared that if he admits he likes your product, you will now use it against them to pressure him to bar. And that is really, really crucial because once he admits he likes the product, it now sets you up for greatness with the patent that's going to follow. Crucial stuff here. But I want to stop right now. I want you to take a short quiz. This is crucial if you're going to lock this stuff in. And as soon as you're done, hurry back if you can and meet me next lesson, we're cooking with gas. Let's roll. See you soon.
